title: The Limitations of Hard Power in Iran | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: CFR President Michael Froman argues the Iran war (Operation Epic Fury) exposes the limits of US hard power, with three durable consequences: erosion of freedom of navigation (potential Hormuz tolls and a US 'dark fleet'), the leverage of cheap asymmetric drones/mines against expensive platforms, and strained Gulf alliances as bases become liabilities and partners hedge toward Russia and China.

insights.claim: Reopening the Strait of Hormuz is now the top issue and central sticking point in Iran negotiations, having been open and free before the conflict began February 28.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: A contested chokepoint elevates the structural risk premium on seaborne energy; Hormuz status is the key variable for oil through-traffic.
insights.quote: The Strait of Hormuz was open and free prior to the start of this phase of the conflict with Iran on February 28. Now, reopening it is the top issue and central sticking point in the negotiations with Iran.

insights.claim: The US is reportedly sneaking as many as fifteen oil and gas tankers per day through Hormuz sailing 'dark' without lights or transponders, versus a pre-crisis status quo of more than fifty.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Covert transits at under 30 percent of normal volume confirm energy supply remains severely constrained regardless of headline diplomacy.
insights.quote: those clandestine strait transits, reported to be as many as fifteen oil and gas tankers per day, would pale in comparison to the pre-crisis status quo of more than fifty.

insights.claim: A toll or 'environment fee' on Hormuz vessels now appears plausible and could prove contagious, inviting copycat levies from countries astride other chokepoints like Indonesia (Malacca), Morocco (Gibraltar), or England (Dover).
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Normalizing chokepoint tolls would permanently raise global shipping and energy transit costs; watch for precedent-setting fee structures.
insights.quote: This could be an expensive lesson in the value of the rules-based system, particularly if it proves contagious as a source of deterrence, leverage, or finance by other countries who are blessed by geography to sit astride a strategic passage

insights.claim: The US is adopting the 'dark fleet' tactics pioneered by China, Russia, North Korea, and Iran to evade sanctions, signaling further erosion of the rules-based maritime order it built.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: When the order's architect adopts evasion tactics, sanctions enforcement and maritime transparency weaken globally, complicating compliance for shippers and insurers.
insights.quote: it’s ironic that the United States is now taking a page out of the playbook of China, Russia, North Korea, and even Iran, whose so-called “dark fleets” pioneered these techniques

insights.claim: Even after a massive US strike campaign degraded its forces, Iran's cheap drones, mines, and missiles wreaked havoc on a critical waterway and US military assets, showing technological supremacy does not guarantee victory.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Cheap attritable systems can neutralize expensive platforms—a cost-asymmetry thesis bullish for low-cost drone/counter-drone defense tech and bearish for legacy exquisite systems.
insights.quote: even in its weakened state, Iran’s deployment of relatively inexpensive drones, mines, and missiles has managed to wreak havoc on one of the world’s most critical waterways

insights.claim: The US 5th Fleet's Task Force 59 deployed an unmanned surface vessel in combat for the first time, underscoring accelerating enthusiasm for autonomous warfare, mirrored from the Russia-Ukraine war.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: First combat use of unmanned surface vessels marks a procurement inflection toward autonomous/attritable systems—a structural tailwind for defense-autonomy suppliers.
insights.quote: the U.S. 5th Fleet’s Task Force 59 deployed an unmanned surface vessel in combat for the first time—in this instance, to rescue two service members.

insights.claim: Less sophisticated adversaries can threaten exquisite US platforms, as shown when an Iranian drone hit a US Apache helicopter.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Vulnerability of high-value platforms to cheap drones reinforces a shift in defense spend toward survivable, distributed, and unmanned systems.
insights.quote: the incident also highlights the extent to which attritable systems fielded by less sophisticated adversaries can threaten exquisite U.S. platforms—in this case, an Apache helicopter being hit by an Iranian drone.
insights.locator: This mission should rightfully be heralded

insights.claim: Before the war the US stationed roughly forty thousand troops across at least nineteen Middle East sites, an arrangement central to deterrence and influence that the conflict has now tested by turning bases into targets.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: If the basing compact frays, US regional posture and deterrence weaken, raising long-run instability in energy-producing Gulf states.
insights.quote: the United States stationed some forty thousand troops across at least nineteen sites throughout the Middle East.

insights.claim: Iranian missiles and drones struck Qatar's Ras Laffan gas complex, refineries in Kuwait and Saudi Arabia, Kuwait airport fuel tanks, a Bahrain desalination plant, and three Amazon data centers in the UAE and Bahrain.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Direct hits on Gulf energy and data infrastructure (including hyperscaler data centers) quantify physical war risk to regional LNG, refining, and cloud capacity.
insights.quote: Iranian missiles and drones struck Qatar’s Ras Laffan gas complex, refineries in Kuwait and Saudi Arabia, fuel tanks at Kuwait’s international airport, and a desalination plant in Bahrain, along with three Amazon data centers in the United Arab Emirates and Bahrain
insights.locator: And that is to say nothing of the damage

insights.claim: The US may emerge from the war with diminished basing rights as longtime Gulf allies hedge their bets, inviting in Russia and China or striking their own accommodations with Iran.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Gulf hedging toward China and Russia would reshape regional alignment, energy flows, and arms/infrastructure contracts away from US interests.
insights.quote: the United States may emerge from this war facing the prospect of diminished basing rights, and of longtime allies hedging their bets—whether by inviting in other powers like Russia and China or by striking their own accommodations with Iran

insights.claim: Froman argues the Iran experiment (Operation Epic Fury) shows it is one thing to reshape limited operations like Venezuela's Operation Absolute Resolve, but another to reshape the geography of power across an entire region by force.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: The thesis that kinetic might-makes-right strategy hits real constraints implies persistent instability and a higher baseline geopolitical risk premium.
insights.quote: it is one thing to change the rules of the game, and quite another to win it.
